title: National Security Legislation
description: A collection of RAND research on the topic of National Security Legislation
text:
Nationale veiligheidsrisico's in greenfieldinvesteringen: Lessen voor investeringstoetsing in Nederland: [National Security risks for greenfield investments: Lessons for investment review in the Netherlands]
This report examines national security risks of greenfield versus brownfield investments in the Netherlands and Europe, assessing threats, review mechanisms, and policy options for balancing economic interests and security in investment screening.
Averting a Robot Catastrophe: Preparing for Converging Trends in Robotics and Frontier AI
The authors assess how the potential proliferation of robotic embodiments of artificial general intelligence could introduce significant vulnerabilities, although the benefits of advanced robotic capabilities likely outweigh their associated risks.
Incentives for U.S.-China Conflict, Competition, and Cooperation Across Artificial General Intelligence’s Five Hard National Security Problems
The authors of this paper assess prospects for conflict, competition, and cooperation between the United States and China across five national security problems posed by artificial general intelligence.
Organizing for Army Financial Audit Success: Steering Army Organizations Toward Financial Audit Success
The U.S. Army has been working toward achieving a clean financial audit for decades but has yet to show significant progress in doing so. RAND conducted a study of Army financial management organizations to identify opportunities for change.
How Federal Funding Terms and Conditions Could Encourage Safe Artificial Intelligence Development
In this paper, the authors examine policy and regulatory opportunities available to the U.S. federal government to enforce safety and security requirements on artificial intelligence systems supported by U.S. government funds.
Addressing the National Security Risks of Bulk Data in the Age of AI
There is an urgent need for a more nuanced and empirically grounded approach to data regulation, one that recognizes how seemingly innocuous data points can, when amassed in large quantities, pose substantial risks to national security.
Taking the Measure of AI and National Security
Transformative technologies should not be abandoned due to their risks. Instead, we should focus on understanding the threats, implementing necessary countermeasures, and utilizing the tools in a safer and more secure manner.
An Assessment of Two-Year Probationary Period Usage Within the Defense Civilian Workforce: A Report Prepared for the U.S. Department of Defense in Compliance with Section 1102 of the Fiscal Year 2020 National Defense Authorization Act
This report summarizes the results of a congressionally mandated independent review of the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)'s use of the two-year probationary period for new appointments to the Senior Executive Service and competitive service.
RAND Review: July-August 2023
Features explore ways to address the opioid crisis with a whole-systems approach and how truth decay — the diminishing role of facts and analysis in public life — is putting U.S. national security at risk.
Age of Danger: Keeping America Safe in an Era of New Superpowers, New Weapons, and New Threats
An urgent look at how America's national security machine went astray and how it fails to keep us safe — and what we can do to fix it.
Preventing Intelligence Leaks: Let's Start Over
The United States badly needs a new secrecy paradigm to protect classified information, and one that also improves government transparency. Our archaic system for keeping classified information secure is terminally flawed, and no amount of triage tinkering can hope to fix it.
